data.title: Comtech Is Changing But Remains Risky And Very Unprofitable
data.description:
Comtech Telecommunication (CMTL) has changed significantly since I last covered it in October 2024.

After an agreement with some shareholders, the company replaced part of its Board, Chairman, and CEO in late 2024 and early 2025.

The company's strategy now focuses on nurturing Emergency (now called Allerium) and restructuring Satellite. Although understanding underlying operations is hard because of massive adjustments and expenses, it seems that underlying operations are improving in Satellite, with Emergency more clearly performing. There has been no comment I could find on whether the company continues to plan the sale of the now-called Allerium segment, or whether that will become the core breadwinner now.

Financially, the situation remains hairy, with massive interest and preferred dividend costs that are hard to cover even with optimistic assumptions about the businesses. Potentially, a business sale could change this.

Overall, I believe the name remains unattractive considering the challenges ahead (mainly financial), except for very speculative readers. I maintain a Hold rating.

Governance changes

CMTL has gone through several CEO changes in the past three years. The current company CEO is the fifth since 2022.

In late 2024, the company reached an agreement with some shareholders (two of whom are historical managers at the company, including the 1976-2022 CEO), to make changes to the board.

In November 2024, the Chairman was replaced, and in January 2025, the new Chairman also took the role of CEO. The new Chairman/CEO comes from a history of turnarounds and governance improvement initiatives, as mentioned in the corresponding press releases and on his LinkedIn page.

Restructuring and strategy

Over the past four quarters, the new management team has established the outlook of a strategy for the business (most relevant reviews in the Q1 and Q4 calls).

Operationally, the company has put most of the problems in the Satellite division. These problems included low margin sales and contracts, a lack of alignment between sales and the business, a lack of cost control, problems at procurement/inventory, and a lack of technological leadership (leading to obsolete products and dead ends in development). I had commented on some of these problems in my October 2024 article, especially the low margins of the business (which, in my opinion, are unfortunately more structural than believed).

This division faced restructuring this year, with divestments of small subsidiaries (mentioned in the latest release) and the deprioritization of some product lines (50% of slow-moving products abandoned, according to the Q4 call). This all carried massive costs, as reported on the Q4 earnings release, including $80 million in impairment of goodwill and intangibles, $15 million in restructuring, and $16 million in inventory write-offs.

The Terrestrial & Wireless division, which mainly focused on emergency call networks (NG-911 products), was renamed Allerium. Whereas before the Board/management changes, the company had released that it had plans to sell this business, the new administration has not mentioned the topic again. The business remains the 'crown-jewel' with no comments about it being problematic, but rather a potential for growth. This was already known last year, and one of the reasons the company wanted to sell the business.

Where the businesses stand today

It is hard to understand the position of the businesses today, in particular for Satellite, because of the restructuring impacts (including both expenses and the loss of sales).

Still, from Q4 data, we can observe that Satellite revenues are flat to slightly down from last year, and on a sequential basis, but with a significant improvement in both adjusted EBITDA ($6.3 million) and operating income for the business, moving to positive territory in operating income for the quarter ($3.3 million). The quarter did not include significant restructuring (as reported) but did include accelerated shipments. This could indicate a breakeven business, but the potential for growth in contracts, sales, and profitability is not clear yet.

Allerium is easier to understand, growing at approximately low-teens YoY, with expanding profitability. Yearly adjusted EBITDA is close to $48 million, and annualized from Q4, it is closer to $55 million.

Adjusted EBITDA is a better measure for both businesses because the company amortizes a lot of intangible assets from acquisitions, which are not reflected in CAPEX/Investment. For FY25, the company recorded $33 million in D&A (on top of almost $80 million in impairments), but then only invested $8.5 million in PP&E.

The problem remains financing

Assuming Satellite now operates at $25 million in adjusted EBITDA figures annualized, and Allerium's $55 million annualized from Q4, we reach $80 million in segment-level adjusted EBITDA. From that, we need to remove corporate expenses not included above (10-K under segment information), reaching $47 million. If we remove this, and $10 million in CAPEX potentially, this leaves $23/25 million in operating income at the aggregate level (adding back amortization).

However, the company pays $20 million in preferred dividends alone per quarter ($80 million annualized), and almost $50 million in yearly interest. Even assuming the company is already operating (underlying all the restructuring, proxy expenses, and impairment) at operating profitability, the name still has to multiply operating income 5x to cover its cost of financing.

After several modifications (under Preferred Shares in the 10-K), the conditions remain similar to last year (9% interest PIK, 7.75% in cash, and conversion at $8 at the option of the holders, plus optionality for the company after 2027).

Here, there's a problem in the calculation I have not been able to solve. The recorded redemption value of the preferred share is $204 million, which does not add up with the $20 million in dividends recorded quarterly (instead of annually, as it should be with 9% interest). Assuming dividends were $20 million annually instead of quarterly, the yearly interest + dividend burden would decrease significantly (to $70 million from $130 million), but the company would still need a 3x increase in operating income to cover them.

It seems that, barring a massive (3/5x) improvement in the business, the company's only choices are the sale of part of the business or the conversion of the preferred shares, which at current stock prices (it would make no sense for preferred shareholders to convert at the current strike of $8) would imply an increase of 250% in common shares, or a dilution of 72% to current common stockholders.

The name is too risky until more clarity is provided

As explained above, currently, despite operational improvements, Comtech cannot remotely generate any income for common stockholders.

Assuming a 3/5x improvement in operational profitability seems extremely unrealistic today, based on the year of restructuring, which has not yet shown a change of that magnitude (or any significant magnitude to be certain).

In my opinion, the most probable path ahead is a capital restructuring, which could come from the conversion of preferred, or from the sale of a business.

This last point is where the potential speculative value of the stock lies. Toying with numbers, and assuming different EBITDA multiples for Allerium, this is how the cap table would remain.

Multiple 5x 7x 10x
Sale $275M $385M $550M
Preferred ($205M) $0 $0 $0
Debt ($211M) $141M $31M $0
Remaining to eq $0 $0 $135M

The above shows that, barring a high multiple on EBITDA, the common equity does not receive anything from the sale of Allerium, and that not even all debt can be paid down (although the burden would decrease significantly).

The remaining business, Satellite, however, may not be profitable at all, because from the current adjusted EBITDA ($25 million annualized), it cannot cover current corporate and CAPEX (at ~$55 million). It is not clear how much of these costs would remain post-divestment.

This means the post-divestment company is, quite probably, unprofitable, implying the only value for common stockholders comes from a high sale price for Allerium (if that sale is carried out at all).

For these reasons, I believe the name remains too risky and uncertain. I would reconsider after a change in capital structure (equitization of preferred) or after a change in the businesses owned/divested.

For the time being, I maintain a hold.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPYI: Save On Taxes With A Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
The NEOS S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) is one of the most talked about covered call funds on Seeking Alpha. The fund routinely attracts large numbers of comments from investors eager for high income and some upside. Based on the stock’s year-to-date total return, the fund does appear interesting, as it has slightly outperformed the S&P 500 while being far less volatile than the index.

So, is SPYI worth the investment today?

I would say that it has several possible places in a sensible investor’s portfolio.

First, if you have high income needs but don’t want to be forced to think about stock sales, the yield itself could be considered a positive. At 11.7%, yield has been the main driver of this fund’s gains. Despite academic theory stating that dividends have no advantage over capital gains, research has repeatedly found that dividends are more predictable and consistent than capital gains. So, for retirees and others with high income needs, a fund like SPYI could make a lot of sense.

Secondly, and perhaps more importantly, covered call funds smooth out volatility to a considerable degree. For investors with liquidity needs, this is important. In conventional, textbook finance, volatility is synonymous with risk. Some investors disagree with this definition, seeing true risk as the probability of a permanent loss of capital. Regardless of whether you agree with the “risk = volatility” equation or not, it’s undeniable that volatility is risk for those with high liquidity needs (i.e., retirees depending on portfolio returns to pay living expenses).

So, there is a strong case to be made for certain investors to hold covered call funds. Knowing this, why should an investor choose to invest in SPYI, when everybody knows about the more popular covered call funds like the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and the JPMorgan NASDAQ Equity Premium ETF (JEPQ)?

A big part of it is the fact that SPYI enjoys considerable tax efficiency. The fund uses Section 1256 contracts to change how its option income is taxed. Section 1256 contracts are taxed 60% as long-term capital gains, 40% as short-term capital gains. Long-term capital gains are taxed less than short term capital gains, so for anybody with mostly short-term capital gains, section 1256 contracts result in lower taxes. Option income is normally taxed as capital gains rather than income, and covered call funds trade fairly frequently. So, the use of 1256 contracts lowers SPYI’s tax burden compared to other similar funds. This is one advantage that SPYI has but other covered call funds don’t have.

Is SPYI’s tax efficiency alone enough to justify including the fund in your portfolio compared to another covered call fund? If your tax rate is high enough, and you live in the U.S., then probably yes. SPYI does have somewhat higher fees than JPMorgan’s (JPM) covered call funds do, but its tax efficiency edge is much greater. SPYI’s AUM is 0.68%, while JEPI’s is 0.35%. Classing 60% of your capital gain as long term, results in an absolute maximum 20% tax on that 60%, vs 37% if it’s a short term capital gain. The savings here is much greater than the fee gap between SPYI and JEPI.

When I last covered the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF, I rated the fund a buy because of its high income potential and hedging benefits. Since then, the fund has risen 2.8% and delivered a 7% total return. The results seen since my last article came out were satisfactory. In this article I explain why SPYI continues to be an attractive option for income oriented investors, as well as those who have an ambivalent view on the market.

Hedging Benefits

Though most investors are aware of covered calls’ income payouts, fewer are aware of the fact that such funds also serve as powerful hedging tools. Because they provide so much income, covered call funds provide a hedge against downside equal to the yield on the fund.

It’s here that SPYI really shines. According to Seeking Alpha Quant, the fund has an 11.7% dividend yield. That means that an investor holding this fund is protected against up to an 11.7% drawdown in the S&P 500. Whereas an investor in the S&P 500 that saw his holdings fall 11.7% over the span of a year would be down 11.7% (less a tiny dividend), the SPYI holder would be about even. So, SPYI is useful in an investor’s portfolio as a partial hedge against the S&P 500’s downside risk.

Many investors believe that the downside risk in the S&P 500 is considerable today. The index trades at historically high multiples, with a 30 P/E ratio, a 5.5 price to book ratio, and a 40 Shiller P/E ratio. These multiples are among the highest in history. The current Shiller P/E ratio is the second highest in history, while the P/E ratio is among the four highest ever. It’s a pretty richly valued market. But there is a case to be made that the high-growth and future profits generated by AI are/will be worth it. This is a situation where there’s a case for going long, as well as a case for wanting a lower price—in other words, a situation where hedged exposure is ideal.

With SPYI, you get a partial hedge against the S&P 500’s downside risk, but not a full one. Once an 11.7% drawdown is exceeded, the investor starts taking losses (assuming he’s been holding a year). If, on the other hand, the investor buys protective puts with a strike price equaling the price at which he bought the S&P 500, he fully protects his position, less a small option premium. So, if hedging is your absolute #1 priority, long puts are the way to go.

Income Potential

As mentioned previously, SPYI has an 11.7% dividend yield. That is based on the dividends paid in the trailing 12-month period. Are the fund’s dividends risky, or likely to cease being paid?

Based on the fund’s dividend history, the answer to that question would seem to be no. As you can see in the image below, SPYI’s dividends have been paid every month since inception without interruption, and have been rising over time.

SPYI’s section 1256 contracts also help you to maximize your take-home dividend income. With such contracts, 60% of your gains are taxed as long-term gains. That means the maximum tax on them is 20%, whereas you'd pay up to 37% on the same dividends if taxed as short-term gains, if you are in the top federal tax bracket. If you get $10,000 per year worth of dividends from SPYI, the difference is $1,200 in taxes vs. $2,220—so you save $1,020 with SPYI’s tax-efficient contracts!

On a forward-looking basis, it is possible that SPYI’s dividend income will decline. Covered call funds write calls on their underlying portfolio. If the fund’s holdings shrink, then it will have fewer assets on which to write calls, and that will gradually reduce the premium income over time. So, downside risk in SPYI’s portfolio can also impact its distribution yield.

Fees and Expenses

One of the negatives with SPYI is the fees and expenses. They are higher than average, at 0.68%. Other covered call funds have fees as low as 0.35%. Additionally, the fund has a 0.04% bid-ask spread, which again is higher than that of many similar funds. 0.04% might not sound like much, but if you’re trading in and out of SPYI frequently, the fees could really start to add up.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on the NEOS S&P 500(R) High Income ETF is it’s a great way to hedge against the risk and uncertainty in the current market, while generating high income to boot. High yield, low volatility, and tax efficiency—this fund has it all.

data.title: My Prediction: Back To All Time Highs Before Year End
data.description:
In my last macro coverage, I downgraded to Hold ahead of the December FOMC meeting, warning that a further rate cut was unlikely. Seeing the deteriorating sentiment after Nvidia obliterated earnings, I was sceptical whether markets would recover their downward trend from last week.

I was dead wrong. I now upgrade my rating back to a strong buy as the probability of a 25 bps cut has risen materially, and incoming data, in my view, now favours a constructive setup into year-end.

First, Fed officials have turned more dovish, with commentary from Waller and Williams suggesting that policy is modestly restrictive and that rates could move lower in the near term. The market is now pricing in an 85% chance for a rate cut, up from just 30% last week.

Second, the latest labour market and price data suggest cooling demand without an inflation re-acceleration. In fact, after the September PPI print, I argue that tariffs have zero to minimal impact on inflation. This supports a December cut.

Third, I believe a rate cut in December would likely lead to a potential breakout to new all-time highs. In the meantime, I expect the broader markets to consolidate close to the October highs.

A Roller Coaster Ride

Since my last article, there have been quite a few developments that point to a December 25 bps cut. The CME Group is now suggesting an 85% chance for another rate cut vs 30% just a week ago.

What caused this turnaround?

First, Fed officials have shifted their tone, suggesting a more dovish stance. Last Monday, Fed Governor Christopher Waller made the following comments on Fox News:

most of the private sector and anecdotal data that we've gotten is that nothing has really changed. The labor market is soft. It's continuing to weaken

That comment is dovish. Furthermore, NY Fed President John Williams said last week that interest rates can fall "in the near term". He further added:

I view monetary policy as being modestly restrictive...Therefore, I still see room for a further adjustment in the near term to the target range for the federal funds rate to move the stance of policy closer to the range of neutral

In response, some analyst firms moved their calls for a rate cut forward to December. This reignited the much-needed optimism in the broader markets, which had lost its momentum since the start of November.

In fact, it's not a coincidence that since last Friday (after Christopher Waller's comments), the markets have rebounded quite aggressively, with four consecutive green days. Notice the high beta between the probability of a rate cut in December and the S&P 500.

Favourable Data For A Rate Cut

In my last piece, I covered why the September employment situation data was favourable for a rate cut. To remind readers, the unemployment rate came in 10 bps above the consensus, now sitting at 4.4%, the highest since late 2021.

The PPI print showed a 0.3% m/m increase, in line with the consensus. In my view, the PPI data suggest that tariffs are having a minimal impact on prices. On a TTM basis, the demand PPI was +2.7%, the same as in August (when tariffs were paused). In fact, the core PPI (i.e., final demand PPI excluding food, energy, and trade-services) rose only 0.1% m/m, below the consensus of 0.2%.

The weekly jobless claims data showed that continuing claims were 1.96 million (+7,000), suggesting that people on benefits are taking longer to find jobs, consistent with slower hiring.

Furthermore, retail sales data for September showed an increase of just 0.2% m/m (vs. expectations of 0.4% m/m), after a 0.6% increase in August. In my view, this suggests that consumer demand is slowing down, which is a dovish signal.

Overall, I see the recent economic data favourable for a rate cut in December.

Risks

The data discussed above (except for weekly jobless claims) is for the month of September. As you may know, the October data will not be released due to the government shutdown. There is a risk here that the developments in October could be unfavourable for a rate cut. If unemployment improved and inflation spiked since the month of October, then the November release could spook market participants.

The November 2025 Employment Situation report was delayed after the Fed meeting and is now scheduled for December 16, 2025. The same happened with the November CPI data. In my last coverage, I noted that this report will be released on December 10 (the second day of the FOMC meeting). This release has now been delayed to December 18.

Looking Ahead

Going into the December 9-10 FOMC meeting, I see several economic indicators to keep a close eye on. Next week, I will closely monitor the November ISM Manufacturing and Services PMI. If I see services, employment, and new orders soft, that would be an indication of cooling demand & hiring. On the other hand, if prices paid for components jump, that’s a warning that inflation in services (the sticky part of inflation) could come in hot for the month of November.

In my view, the single most important economic release before the FOMC meeting will be the Personal Income and Outlays report for September. This data will give the Fed insight into core PCE inflation. If the monthly core PCE data comes in closer to 0.1–0.2%, that would be consistent with disinflation. However, anything hotter than 0.3% m/m would make me sweat.

Overall, I believe the setup going into year-end is favourable. From a price action perspective, I expect the broader markets to retest the October all-time highs, and I expect a breakout after the FOMC rate cut decision on December 10.

data.title: AIEQ: The Human Vs. AI Stock-Picking Test
data.description:
AI is expected to replace a lot of jobs. But can it replace your portfolio manager and even you as a trade? I have no idea, but it's worth seeing if a fund that uses AI can indeed outperform. Leveraging the IBM Watson platform and using EquBot's machine learning and language processing algorithms, the Amplify Ai Powered Equity ETF (AIEQ) can analyze millions of data points in seconds and execute immediately, making it potentially quicker to react than a human to market opportunities.

It's AI meets stock picking in an ETF. Sounds great on paper, but in practice? It turns out being passive beats artificial intelligence.

A Hard Cycle to Beat a Dumb Index

When we look at the price ratio of the AIEQ to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), we see that the ratio peaked in 2021. Since then, the S&P 500 has significantly outperformed. Stock picking, done by machine and algorithm, doesn't seem to work (at least at first glance).

The S&P 500's rise over the last several years has been powered by a narrow set of mega-cap technology stocks. With a few giants accounting for almost half the market's return, any strategy that doesn't remain highly concentrated in them has performed poorly. AIEQ's model is not a simple mirror of the index; it rotates based on an in-house scoring system

When we look at the holdings of AIEQ, we see that it has owned some of the marquee tech names, but not in aggressive proportions. AI stock picking apparently has a hard time when the odds just don't favor breadth momentum, and instead when we are in an environment where market returns are extremely concentrated.

How the Machine Actually Chooses Stocks

At the heart of AIEQ's decision-making is a quantitative engine that runs all day long. The model ingests financial statements, earnings calls, news flow, and market sentiment from thousands of companies. It also ranks equities and chooses a slim slice, usually between 30 and 70 holdings, for the portfolio. Rebalancing is frequent. While traditional managers may ease into and out of positions, the algorithm can dramatically change holdings depending on its output.

In practice, that results in a portfolio that acts not so much like a steady index alternative but rather more like a fast-moving hedge fund. Over time, AIEQ has been willing to get into hot areas, and then quickly rotate away. It has loaded up on Big Tech when momentum favored those names, then sharply reduced exposure when its signals turned. At other times, it has tilted into mid-cap growth, cybersecurity, medical technology, or energy stocks, at times benefitting from upside but also being whipsawed.

Why AI Seems to Fall Short in Some Market Regimes

A lot of investors believe AI should do well in the age of an AI-driven boom. But the reverse has been true. The algorithms at the helm of funds such as AIEQ have to be trained on historical data. They're good at finding patterns that fit the past, not so much ones that are genuinely new. Imagine a sudden disruption, like the explosion in interest in generative A.I. or an unexpected shock to monetary policy: the machine has no analogous historical situation to consider.

Algorithms are based on what already is. That distinction matters. When the policy stance of the Federal Reserve changed swiftly, when tech valuations went ballistic, when investor psychology flipped virtually overnight, the model often lagged. It wasn't programmed to foresee the type of breakneck regime change that characterized certain periods over the last several years.

Another headwind is structural. High turnover in AIEQ means higher trading costs. Its management fee is dramatically higher than that of an index fund. Even if you theoretically can identify clear winners, those frictions blunt the edge.

The Promise, And Setting Realistic Expectations

That's not to say AIEQ is a failure. In many respects, it's still an interesting experiment. The model has shown it is capable of handling more information than any human team could. It has exposed lesser-known names that happened to be out-performers. It has helped it steer clear of some large drawdowns that bigger, more static portfolios have experienced. And, in good times, it has provided absolute returns.

The issue is consistency. Beating one of the most challenging benchmarks to outperform, the S&P 500, is a high bar. It's not easily cleared by live human managers over long horizons. No machine has yet proved superior.

When a lot of sectors participate in gains, the ETF tends to hold up well. In a year where markets are reliant on a handful of massive winners, as in 2025, a diversified AI model will almost always lag.

Final Takeaway

AIEQ is still very much writing the playbook for what AI-driven investing can be. It provides a lens into how machine learning could work in the real world - taking in information, adapting independently, and removing emotional bias from decision-making. For investors, the fund is less a surefire way to beat the market than a tool for getting exposure to an interesting approach that could pay off in certain circumstances.

data.title: JOYY: Growth In Bigo Unit May Drive Re-Rating
data.description:
JOYY Inc. (JOYY), a Singapore-based social media company, shows signs of reviving growth following quarters of stagnation. The potential growth catalyst adds to the stock’s appeal as a cigar butt.

Revenue has been climbing QoQ for two consecutive quarters, driven by higher sales of both livestreaming and advertising at the Bigo unit. The management expressed optimism about resuming annual growth in 2026 at a Nov. 20 earnings call.

The stock has returned about 50% since I initiated coverage in May, recommending it as a deep value play. Although JOYY has become less undervalued, I maintain my ‘buy’ rating based on a model of discounted future dividends, while treating potential growth as free optionality.

Overview

Founded in 2005 in Guangzhou of south China, the company operates social media and entertainment platforms including Bigo Live, Likee, imo, and Hago. Offering “interactive and immersive entertainment,” JOYY has about 266 million monthly active mobile users across these platforms as of September, according to its 3Q25 report.

Expanding globally, the company moved its headquarters to Singapore in 2019. In the first nine months of this year, JOYY derived the majority of its revenue from developed countries like the US, Singapore, Japan, South Korea, and the UK, while China accounted for about one-tenth of the pie.

3Q highlights

The biggest takeaway from third-quarter results is sales improvement, especially in the Bigo unit, which accounts for more than 80% of company revenue. I find it encouraging because the Bigo weakness has been a key risk to my investment thesis for this cigar-butt stock.

Revenue at the company level rose 6.4% QoQ, with livestreaming up 3.5% and ads up 17.1% vs the second quarter. It was the second consecutive quarter of sequential improvement. Meanwhile, the management’s strategy of diversifying income seems to be working, with livestreaming representing 72% of sales in 3Q25, down from 87% two years ago.

CEO Ting Li expressed confidence a few times about returning to growth soon on the Nov. 20 call.

Based on the trends we are already observing across our major businesses, we have very clear visibility into the '26 for the group's return to positive year-over-year revenue growth, and we have very strong confidence in that. (Ting Li, Nov. 20 earnings call)

Bigo revenue rose 6.7% QoQ, including a 3.5% increase in livestreaming and a 19.7% jump in ads. Total paying users of Bigo and the average revenue per user both improved QoQ.

The number of high-paying users increased faster than the rest of Bigo’s customer mix, thanks to optimization of a tiered system for user benefits, Li said on the earnings call.

Shareholder returns

From January to mid November, the company has paid out $148 million in dividends and spent $88.6 million repurchasing shares. The dividends and buybacks combined account for 7.3% of JOYY’s market cap as of Nov. 26.

Since 2020, JOYY has repurchased shares for more than $1.2 billion, equivalent to nearly 40% of its current market cap. Under a buyback program for 2025-27, the company still has an unutilized quota of about $200 million.

We believe we are still substantially undervalued, and we will remain firmly committed to actively utilize our outstanding share repurchase program.” (Fuyong Liu, VP of Finance, Nov. 20 earnings call)

The management also reaffirmed its commitment to a dividend plan worth $600 million in total for 2025-27.

A strong balance sheet supports JOYY’s plan to continue returning value to investors. As of September, the company holds $3.3 billion of cash and short-term investments, versus a negligible $35 million of debts.

Valuation

Seeing is believing. Despite the management’s increasingly bullish tone, I would wait for another one or two quarters for more evidence of a solid turnaround. For now, let’s still treat JOYY as a cigar butt and value it based on conservative estimates of future dividends or cash flows.

The Dividend Discount Model is appropriate here given the company’s commitment to paying stable dividends, its clean balance sheet, and its lack of growth so far. The method is grounded on the theory that a firm should be valued at the sum of all its future dividends discounted back to the present.

Based on JOYY’s track record and current dividend plan, I expect the company to distribute at least $200 million a year, while assuming zero growth for its dividends. At an 8% cost of equity, the stream of annual payments would equal a present value of $2.5 billion, which should be added to the company’s cash pile for a fair market cap of $5.8 billion. With a 15% margin of safety, the fair market cap would drop to about $4.9 billion, implying a target price of $96.7 per ADR share.

Key risks

The projected YoY growth for Bigo, or the company as a whole, has yet to materialize. We should take the management’s optimism with a pinch of salt. Volatility in Bigo’s revenue may call into question the sustainability of JOYY’s dividends and thereby my investment thesis.

Secondly, the company’s SaaS unit is still running at operating losses. While the business line accounts for less than one-tenth of revenue, it may become a drag on profitability. In addition, weak consumer sentiment in developed countries like the US and the UK, among JOYY’s key markets, could delay its recovery.

Conclusion

After stagnating for quarters, JOYY is likely to return to growth soon, thanks to improvement in its Bigo unit. Dividends and buybacks remain strong, supported by a healthy balance sheet and the company's commitment to shareholder returns. Despite a strong rally since I initiated coverage in May, I maintain a buy rating based on discounted future dividends. While JOYY has the potential of becoming a growth stock, I’m treating it as a cigar butt for now, watching for more evidence of a solid turnaround.

data.title: Amer Sports: Solid Performance That Dismissed My Bearish Concerns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Amer Sports (AS) was a hold rating because I was still afraid of the risks associated with the Tibet backlash in China, insider selling trends, and the high valuation. After looking at the Q3 numbers, the setup for AS has gotten a lot better. It didn't just post a strong print; it dismissed the key risks I was worried about. With China accelerating and Arc'teryx accelerating, the fundamentals look far cleaner today. I upgrade to buy.

3Q25 Results Update

AS delivered a solid Q3 that dismissed my bearish view previously. Revenue came in at $1.76 billion, up 30% y/y (28% in constant currency), and well ahead of consensus. Every segment and region contributed to this growth, which is good since growth is not driven by a single brand. Outdoor Performance was the outperformer, up 36% y/y to $724 million, and Technical Apparel also did well, growing 31% y/y to $683 million. Even Ball & Racquet grew 16% y/y to $350 million. Adj. gross margin expanded 240 bps to 57.9%, which drove adj. EBIT growth of 41% y/y to $275 million (EBIT margins came in at 15.7%). Bottom line, adj. EPS more than doubled to $0.33, and this solid performance led management to raise guidance across the board, now calling for 23–24% revenue growth and $0.88–$0.92 in adj. EPS.

China Growth Accelerated

The biggest surprise in Q3 was how fast the “fireworks incident” faded off the radar. In my last update, I pointed out the risk that the online backlash in China could hit demand, especially given that Greater China is AS's most profitable region. Fortunately, the numbers came in way better than expected. Growth in Greater China actually accelerated to 47% y/y, up from 42% in Q2. For one, this tells us that Arc'teryx and Salomon brand equity are far stronger than I expected; and two, it also shows that demand is likely structural (like real organic demand) that is not sentiment driven (which could easily disappear whenever sentiment turns negative).

My take is that demand momentum has continued into Q4, and there are two data points to support my view. Firstly, management noted that while there was some softness in early October because of warm weather and the incident, the trends have already “rebounded.” Secondly, the fact that management didn’t bake any negative impact into their Q4 guide (and even explicitly said strong momentum continues) is a great hint that momentum continues (the earnings call was in November, so they basically know the first 5 to 6 weeks of Q4 have performed). For the bull equity story, this is a major win. It shows that Chinese consumers are still demanding these brands despite the macro weakness.

Arc'teryx Omni-Comp Re-Accelerated Significantly

The other concern was whether Arc'teryx could continue to post strong growth as the base got larger. Well, it certainly seems like growth is not slowing down, as Technical Apparel omni-comp sales (which include DTC stores and e-commerce) growth accelerated to 27% in Q3 2025, 12 points above Q2. If growth accelerated by this much, it is as clear as any data can get that underlying demand remains strong and also that the broader “brand billboard” rollout is working (refer to the quote from my previous update below for details). Also, since most of the comp sales growth strength came from traffic (as per management), not ticket or conversion, it further supports the narrative that more people are discovering the brand and showing up in stores.

The story gets better if you consider that markdown levels were unchanged y/y. Basically, this means that the sharp acceleration in growth came from full-price demand, not discounts (further supporting the strong narrative that organic demand is strong). This confirms that Arc'teryx isn't “buying” growth via discounts (which is not sustainable).

The idea is to roughly double Arc'teryx's global store footprint from ~168 locations in 2025 to ~300 by 2030, with a longer-term target of 500–600. Besides the obvious fact that these stores act as distribution, these stores will also act as “brand billboards” that help drive awareness.

I believe this strategy could work because the physical stores are designed to create a "halo" effect that drives more sales in a new market, and not just the sales within that store's four walls. The increased visibility and local presence should also funnel consumers to the e-commerce business, and with higher consumer mindshare and demand, it should also lead to a lift in demand from wholesale partners in the region as well. Therefore, each new store should be seen as a lever that drives demand across multiple channels.

Initial FY 2026 Outlook and Valuation

The initial guide for FY2026 should put to rest the idea that AS growth momentum is slowing. In contrast to that, management guided revenue growth toward the high end of their long-term guide (low double-digit to mid-teens, with 30–70 bps of EBIT margin expansion). I actually think that guide is too conservative, especially when considering that Q3 saw every region accelerate, and Greater China is growing by ~50% y/y. Elsewhere, the Americas also accelerated to 18% y/y in Q3 2025 (vs. 6% in Q2), which is really telling of the brand strength since the consumer demand backdrop is not great in the US. If AS can grow in the high teens in this environment, when rates (suppose the Fed cuts) come down next year (which should help with consumer demand), demand should easily be sustained in the high teens.

All of these matter for the stock because at ~31x forward PE, AS needs to prove it can continue to compound earnings at a high rate, and this guide de-risks that growth outlook. It was also very positive to hear management say that they see no evidence of a slowdown. My bottom line here is that with a solid setup for earnings to compound at >25% for the next few years (if momentum holds, or overall macro conditions get better), the current multiple isn't too stretched. In fact, I could argue it is cheap since Nike trades at 31x forward PE but only has a growth expectation of mid-single-digits.

Risk

I am optimistic that the Q4 guide is a deliberate conservative action by management. But if it's not, then the stock could see heavy selling pressure, as the implied Q4 guide suggests revenue growth decelerating to 17-20% y/y, a drop from Q3's 30% y/y. Inventory also remains elevated (up 28% y/y), so if demand really falls, we could be in a situation where AS needs to start marking down, and that will not be pretty.

Conclusion

AS now has a credible path to compounding earnings at a high-teens to >20% pace, and the early FY26 guide suggests that momentum isn't fading. The China concern I had was also a non-event, and Arc'teryx is also accelerating off a larger base. At ~31x forward P/E, the stock could be argued to be cheap when compared to Nike, which has lower growth but similar multiples. I upgrade to buy.
